videos.title: Análise: Irã ainda demonstra capacidade de desgastar EUA e Trump | WW
videos.description:
Duas aeronaves militares americanas foram abatidas no Irã nesta sexta-feira (3). Uma delas era um caça com dois tripulantes, um deles foi resgatado, mas não se sabe em que estado estaria, enquanto o outro ainda não foi localizado pelos militares americanos, nem pelas forças do Irã. O professor de Ciências Militares da Eceme Sandro Teixeira Moita, o professor de Ciência Política do Berea College Carlos Gustavo Poggio e o professor da NYU Shanghai e da FDC Rodrigo Zeidan comentam o assunto. #CNNBrasil

videos.transcript: Pesquisar transcrição Duas aeronaves militares americanas foram abatidas no Irã nesta sexta-feira. Uma delas era um caça com dois tripulantes. Um deles foi resgatado pelos Estados Unidos, mas não se sabe em que estado ele estaria agora. O outro tripulante ainda não foi localizado nem pelos militares americanos e nem pelas forças do Irã. Vamos ver a reportagem de Mariana Janjá como direto de Washington. A queda de um CA F15 foi o primeiro caso registrado de uma aeronave dos Estados Unidos abatida dentro do território iraniano desde o início da guerra. Um anúncio de recompensa para quem capturasse a tripulação da aeronave foi veiculado pela mídia iraniana. Imagens divulgadas nas redes sociais e geolocalizadas pela CNN, que mostrariam operações de busca e resgate, indicam que o Caça teria caído em uma região montanhosa no sudoeste do país, próximo à planície petrolífera e à fronteira com o Iraque. Também nesta sexta-feira, a mídia do Irã reportou um comunicado do exército do país dizendo que uma outra aeronave americana caiu nas águas do Golfo Pérsico após ter sido alvejada por sistemas de defesa aérea iranianos próximo ao estreito de Ormuz. O piloto conseguiu sejetar e foi resgatado. Os Estados Unidos têm empregado o A10 Wartog no Irã para identificar alvos com precisão e realizar ataques em baixa altitude contra embarcações iranianas na região do Estreito. No pronunciamento na última quarta-feira, Donald Trump disse que o Irã já não tinha mais equipamentos antiaéreos e que os radares do país tinham sido completamente destruídos. Ele afirmou também que os mísseis iranianos tinham sido destruídos ou esgotados, mas relatórios da inteligência sinalizam desafios um mês depois de bombardeios diários junto a Israel. Fontes com acesso às informações destes documentos disseram a CNN que a inteligência americana aponta que cerca de metade dos drones e dos lançadores de mísseis do Irã seguem intactos. Um dos desafios para os Estados Unidos é que o Irã tem levado lançadores para o subsolo, escondendo-os em túneis e cavernas, mas ainda mantendo-os operacionais. Embora as capacidades da marinha oficial do Irã tenham sido praticamente destruídas, a força naval ligada à Guarda Revolucionária, que tem capitaneado o bloqueio iraniano sobre o estreito de Ormus, ainda conservaria metade de suas capacidades, também segundo estes relatórios. Sandro, claro, começo com você, porque o Trump tem dito reiteradamente que a força militar do Irã foi obliterada. Ele usa essa palavra, né, que tem uma uma força de expressão eh grande. Esse essas essas operações de hoje abatendo caça dos Estados Unidos vão na mostram que estão na direção que tá na direção contrária esse diagnóstico de Trump. É isso? Aí, ah, o que a gente vê é o seguinte, toda a defesa aérea, ela é organizada em três camadas: grande altura, média altura e baixa altura, que teria sido destruído tanto pela Força Aérea Americana quanto pela Força Aérea Jailense. Teriam sido os sistemas de média altura e de grande altura. Mas o Trump não é fã de dizer que tem superioridade aérea, obviamente saiu dizendo isso. Ah, e o que a gente acaba vendo é um sentido no qual ah muito claramente a uma atuação americana, talvez um pouco levando em conta essa ideia de uma superioridade aérea. Só que o problema é quando você opera muito próximo ao solo, você corre riscos e você quando corre riscos, você fica exposto inclusive a sistemas portáteis que podem ser operados por um homem famoso MPAD. E tem e o Ian tem a as dezenas de milhares. Então isso é um grande risco e lembra também a ele os custos de uma eventual ação terrestre. Então aqui o que a gente vê muito claramente com as operações de hoje e essa busca frenética, ah, no qual inclusive própria as informações que dão conta que o próprio ADS teria sido danificado e e abatido nas operações para salvar o piloto do F15. Então o que a gente tá vendo muito claramente, Thaís, é que a o custo da guerra apareceu pro Donald Trump. Então ele deve agora perceber isso. Jo, e certamente Donald Trump não tava esperando correr esse risco, né, de ter um militar americano sendo capturado pelo regime. Quais os cenários possíveis da gente traçar numa situação como essa? Aliás, independentemente dele ser capturado ou não pelo regime, essa situação de agora já é uma situação de alta sensibilidade para Trump. Ao mesmo tempo que parece tá eh assistindo a esse jogo de longe parado, já há sinais de que a China tenta se colocar ali com a possibilidade de ser um moderador dessa conversa. Então, o o Irã, assim como a Ucrânia, tem algum tempo, né, tenta vencer pelo atrito e tenta mostrar que o custo da guerra é muito grande. não funcionou muito no caso da Rússia, porque o Putin não é a coisa mais fácil do mundo, mas talvez funcione no caso americano. Afinal de contas, a China é um grande eh importador de petróleo e qualquer problema eh de um petróleo muito alto bate numa economia que é resiliente, mas não tá indo tão bem assim. do mas do ponto de vista geopolítico, eh, vê os inimigos eh dando tiro no próprio pé ou talvez dando tiro no inimigo, eh, não chega a ser no próprio pé, mas vai acabar voltando. Não é a pior coisa do mundo. Mas a China também já tentou fazer esse papel de moderador no caso da Rússia e não conseguiu. É óbvio que a China tem a China a uma economia 10 vezes maior do que a Rússia e poderia eh tentar forçar os russos, embora sejam inimigos históricos, há uma resolução da guerra, talvez não conseguisse, mas no caso do Irã, ela tem muito menos espaço. As relações China e Irã não são não são as mesmas relações eh China e Rússia, mas com certeza a China vai manter contato com todos os lados. Eh, o que de novo, o que as pessoas não entendem é que a China tem uma posição muito simples. Ela reage da forma que tratam ela. Se tratarem como adversário, ela vai ser adversária. videos.title: Análise: O discurso de Trump versus a realidade dos EUA no Irã | WW
videos.description:
O presidente dos Estados Unidos, Donald Trump, afirmou nesta sexta-feira (3) que o vice-presidente JD Vance se concentrará em investigar fraudes em estados governados por democratas em sua nova função de "czar da fraude". O professor de Ciências Militares da Eceme Sandro Teixeira Moita, o professor de Ciência Política do Berea College Carlos Gustavo Poggio e o professor da NYU Shanghai e da FDC Rodrigo Zeidan comentam o assunto. #CNNBrasil

videos.transcript: Pesquisar transcrição Já deixei a pergunta pingando aqui sobre reação política interna do Trump. Falamos um pouco da economia e do seus reflexos. O que que isso pode provocar de reação aí? É, acho que vem bem a calhar a sua pergunta nesse contexto que nós estamos discutindo, porque isso já faz parte das movimentações do Donald Trump se preparando para as eleições de meio de mandato, né, questão da fraude que foi muito no caso de Minnesota, né, que é estado governado pelo Tim Walts, que foi o candidato ao vice-presidente na chapa da Camalar Harris, houve de fato eh uma questão aí que reuniu diversos elementos que foram favoráveis à administração Trump. Primeiro, uma questão eh que há evidências concretas de fraude naquele estado, né, seja de 200, 250 milhões de dólares, né, o que daria para pagar aí algumas horas de guerra contra o Irã. Eh, o segundo elemento é que um estado, como disse, governado por uma figura democrata importante, né, candidato a vice-presidente na chapa da Camala Hersma. E o terceiro elemento é o elemento que isto envolvia questões de migração, principalmente migração por parte da comunidade Somali em Minnesota. Então isso tornou aí um prato cheio paraa administração Trump explorar politicamente vindo eh do Donald Trump, sabendo como ele opera e com base em declarações que ele próprio deu e os estados que ele mencionou, eh a gente tem aí uma um um esse novo cargo do Jevens Vensk, que nada mais é do que reunir mais poder dentro da Casa Branca eh para um tema que já era lidado por uma série de outras agências federais. eh nada mais é do que uma tentativa de eh se criar um fator político eh contra democratas, né, colando os democratas a pesta de que são governadores que participam de programas fraudulentos, em especial os que envolvem imigrantes, eh, e de alguma forma tentar com isso ganhar algum tipo de narrativa para as eleições de meio de mandato. Então, trata-se de uma lógica já bastante conhecida do Donald Trump de ir buscando aí os seus inimigos internos, né? Eh, já avisando o que será uma eleição de meio de mandato que parece que vai ser muito complicada para os republicanos. Ô, ô, Zeidan, na e no bloco passado você falou da coisa do risco e e o que que o investidor vai sair comprando, se vai comprar ou não título público de país eh de país que já foi triple na vida e e e deixou de ser pelo menos não no papel, mas na percepção. videos.title: Análise: FMI reduz projeções de crescimento diante da guerra no Oriente Médio | WW
videos.description:
O FMI (Fundo Monetário Internacional) alertou que a atual disrupção no mercado de petróleo é a maior da história e destacou os impactos da crise para economias menores e dependentes. A âncora e analista de Economia da CNN Thais Herédia, o professor de Ciências Militares da Eceme Sandro Teixeira Moita e o professor de Ciência Política do Berea College Carlos Gustavo Poggio comentam o assunto. #cnnbrasil 

Agora a CNN Brasil está mais perto de você. Ao fazer parte do Clube de Membros, você tem acesso exclusivo e antecipado ao WW Especial, conhece os bastidores dos telejornais e coberturas especiais, recebe indicações relacionadas ao noticiário da nossa equipe de jornalismo, além de interagir diretamente com os apresentadores, participar de debates e enquetes e tirar suas dúvidas sobre o cenário nacional e internacional com os analistas da casa. Assine agora e participe da CNN de um jeito único:    / @cnnbrasil  

Inscreva-se no canal da CNN Brasil no YouTube.

ACOMPANHE A CNN BRASIL TAMBÉM NAS OUTRAS PLATAFORMAS:

Site: https://www.cnnbrasil.com.br/
Facebook:   / cnnbrasil  
Twitter:   / cnnbrasil  
Instagram:   / cnnbrasil  
TikTok:   / cnnbrasil

videos.title: Astronautas da missão Artemis relatam experiência no espaço: 'Extraordinária' | #NewsNoite
videos.description:
A missão Artemis II já deixou a órbita terrestre e segue em trajetória rumo à Lua, com uma viagem estimada em quatro dias até o sobrevoo lunar. Enquanto os astronautas registram imagens impressionantes da Terra a caminho do satélite, o Jornal News Noite analisa os impactos econômicos e logísticos deste marco histórico em uma entrevista exclusiva com Adalberto Febeliano, mestre em Economia do Transporte Aéreo pelo ITA.
            
Inscreva-se no canal para mais notícias:    / @sbtnews   

Acompanhe no site: https://sbtnews.sbt.com.br/  

#sbtnews #notícias #jornalismo #sbt #sbtjornalismo #nasa #lua #terra #exploraçãoespacial

Sobre esse momento histórico, nós conversamos agora com o Adalberto Febeliano, mestre em economia do transporte aéreo pelo ITA. Então, Adalberto, muito boa noite para você. Obrigado pela disponibilidade. Boa noite, Gaer. Sempre uma satisfação falar com vocês do SBT News. Adalberta, essa é a primeira viagem à lua, então a já há mais de 50 anos. É assim que a gente tem que enxergar a economia do espaço que tá começando a se desenvolver agora. E tem eh empresas de lançamento no mundo inteiro, né? Já se fala até e o Brasil já começou inclusive iniciativas nesse sentido, a a começar a se preparar para fazer a investigação dos acidentes espaciais. Então o Brasil já está tentando se inserir nessa economia do espaço. Eh, de que forma o país pode avançar? Quais são as nossas oportunidades nesse cenário? E também o que que é desafio para um país como o nosso entrar nessa corrida empresarial? Olha, eh, lá na década de 30, um sujeito chamado Brigadeiro Casimiro Montenegro, ele intuiu que a aviação podia ser um um um grande negócio no futuro. Então, ele criou uma escola eh no Brasil, o ITA, para formar engenheiros aeronáuticos. criou um centro de pesquisas em São José dos Campos, o CTA, do it do CTA nasceu a Imbraer e a Embraer hoje é o terceiro maior fabricante de aviões do mundo, né? Tirando Estados Unidos e Europa, a Embraer é de longe, mas de muito longe, o maior fabricante de aeronaves civis do mundo. Então tem espaço, né? tem espaço paraa gente ter um um papel protagonista nesse eh eh nesse eh ambiente do comércio do espaço. Mas obviamente precisamos ter alguém com a determinação do brigadeiro Montenegro, porque ele fez disso a a missão da vida dele e colocou o país num num patamar totalmente diferente eh em termos de tecnologia aeronáutica. E a gente tem que achar os mecanismos para repetir isso também com a tecnologia espacial. e me corrija se eu estiver errado, mas acho que também tem uma questão geográfica que pode favorecer o Brasil, a proximidade com a linha do Equador, até pro lançamento de foguetes. videos.title: APOLLO 11 - A HISTÓRIA NÃO CONTADA.
videos.description:
Em 20 de julho de 1969, 600 milhões de pessoas em todo o mundo observaram Neil Armstrong dar os primeiros passos no solo lunar. Com a colaboração da NASA, este documentário revela a história completa dessa missão lendária. A história da Apollo 11 é a crónica da primeira missão espacial bem-sucedida a pousar humanos na Lua, em 20 de julho de 1969, cumprindo um objetivo estabelecido pelos EUA na Guerra Fria. A tripulação, liderada por Neil Armstrong, com Buzz Aldrin e Michael Collins a bordo, partiu da Terra a 16 de julho e regressou oito dias depois, marcando um feito histórico para a humanidade e a tecnologia.

INSCREVA-SE NO CANAL C. ESTÚDIO PARA RECEBER NOVOS VÍDEOS, ATIVE O SININHO, COMPARTILHE COM AMIGOS E DEIXE SEU LIKE.

Acervo de imagens Canal C. Estúdio.
Canal C. Estúdio NÃO RECEBE MONETIZAÇÃO pela postagem e exibição deste conteúdo.
Os autores dos direitos autorais contidos neste documentário, RECEBEM MONETIZAÇÃO através da execução.

videos.title: O QUE ESTÃO ESCONDENDO SOBRE O VOO DA MALAYSIA? ✈️ | GRANDES MISTÉRIOS DA HISTÓRIA
videos.description:
Neste episódio completo da série "Grandes Mistérios da História", vamos rever um dos mistérios aeronáuticos mais espantosos dos últimos tempos: um Boeing 777 que transportava 239 pessoas desapareceu subitamente no Oceano Índico. Quase dez anos depois, o mundo todo continua se perguntando: o que realmente aconteceu com o voo 370 da Malaysia Airlines?

videos.title: Amelia Earhart: O maior mistério da aviação teve um desfecho? | GRANDES MISTÉRIOS DA HISTÓRIA
videos.description:
Em 1937, a pioneira da aviação Amelia Earhart desaparece durante seu ambicioso voo ao redor do mundo. Seu misterioso desaparecimento perto de uma ilha do Pacífico dá origem a décadas de teorias e perguntas sem resposta.

videos.title: 🔥 Mistérios sombrios e o presidente 🇺🇸 | MARATONA: 2 HORAS DE INEXPLICÁVEL COM WILLIAM SHATNER
videos.description:
Duas horas seguidas de INEXPLICÁVEL COM WILLIAM SHATNER! Se ajeite no sofá ou na cama e venha acompanhar os maiores mistérios sem solução da história.

00:00 OS PERDIDOS - Todos os dias alguém desaparece. Porém ao longo da história, existiram alguns casos singulares que confundiram o mundo inteiro. Desde a perda de ícones como Amelia Earhart a grupos inteiros que desapareceram sem deixar vestígios, esses mistérios geram teorias fascinantes, sem respostas definitivas.
42:00 HISTÓRIAS DE PRESIDENTES - Os homens que presidiram os Estados Unidos são lembrados como líderes ilustres da história. No entanto, existem muitas histórias estranhas e misteriosas envolvendo presidentes americanos. Poderá a verdade por detrás destas histórias surpreendentes reescrever o passado da América e alterar o seu futuro?
1:22:15 MONSTROS MISTERIOSOS - No mundo inteiro, tem havido inumeráveis avistamentos de criaturas estranhas que, segundo relatos de diversas testemunhas oculares, só podem ser descritos como monstros incríveis e feras inexplicáveis para a ciência. Poderiam existir realmente monstros terríveis à espreita nas sombras do planeta?

videos.title: Trump’s in a ‘race against time’ as Iran war lies unravel | Scott Lucas
videos.description:
Professor Scott Lucas joins Maddie Hale for a special Saturday edition of The Trump Report to discuss the rescue operation to find a missing US pilot who was ejected from an F-15 shot down by Iran, the IRGC rejects an alleged 48 hour ceasefire proposal by the US and Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth fires three top generals in the midst of a war.  

Welcome to the Trump Report, join this channel to get access to perks - 
   / @presidenttrumpreport  

To listen to the show as a podcast you can find it here on:

Spotify: https://open.spotify.com/show/1p2WhZ6...
Apple podcasts: https://podcasts.apple.com/podcast/id....
videos.transcript: Pesquisar transcrição And it pushes the Trump administration closer closer to that decision that they're going to have to make. They're either going to have to go into talks with the Iranians, which are not an ultimatum because the Iranians will not accept an ultimatum. They're either going to have to go into talks with the Iranians or they're going to have to go in with ground operations. I don't think there's an in between here. And I think that's what yesterday's events highlight. Hello and welcome to the Trump Report. I'm Maddie Hail. I told you guys we'd be delivering you a Saturday, a special Saturday Trump edition every now and then, and so I've kept my promise. So, I'm going to bring in my guest, Professor Scott Lucas of the Clinton Institute University College Dublin. Scott, thank you so much for joining me on this uh I'm looking around. I don't know, it's cloudy here um on Easter holiday, but thank you for joining the Trump Report. Well, yeah, but you brighten up my days, Maddie. So, all is good here and happy Easter for anyone who celebrates. Uh, and happy, I guess, break if you've just got the few days off. That's always exciting. Um, we will be working this morning for you guys because there is so much to discuss. Um, and we're going to get straight into it. So, Scott, I guess let's kick off with this news in the last day. Two US jets, they were shot down over Iran on Friday for the first time in the war. One of the two pilots from the F-15 ejected safely. He was found by US forces. One of them is still missing. The US is now running a a pretty intense rescue operation in very hostile territory. Uh, of course, there's no US bases nearby. There is fears that this one pilot could be captured um by the RGC. This would obviously be a major escalation. There is also, I think it's a £50,000 bounty uh from the IRGC to find him. So if you're a random Iranian and you see him and you bring him to the forces, you get this bounty. Uh Sean Bell, a military analyst, he comes on the Trump report all the time. He said this on Sky News and he said if they are captured, this is before the first one was found. All of a sudden you're potentially into a Gulf War type scenario. Imagine if Iran were able to parade somebody on the streets. How would that leave President Trump? Where would that leave Americans? How could they recover that individual? Now that's exactly what he said to me last Monday when he came on the show. He said the nature of the war will change if an American service member is captured. This scenario practically ended Jimmy Carter's presidency in 1979 to 81. Now, of course, there was a lot more people captured, but we don't know what catalyst will occur from well, sorry, what snowball effect will occur from this. So, what do you think uh Scott, do you really believe this is a race against time? Well, I mean, let's let's talk where we are on this Saturday morning and then we'll talk about where it affects the military and the political sides uh of the US war in Iran. You know, you're the USF-15E uh Strike Eagle fighter jet was downed over southwest Iran early on Friday. Um the Iranians were reporting it fairly quickly. The Americans took quite a bit of time and in fact they haven't they didn't officially acknowledge it. There were just US officials that finally confirmed to the media, yeah, the plane is down. Uh the search and rescue mission uh was immediately carried out. And so even before the Americans confirmed the downing, we were getting the photographs across social media of the US helicopters, including with a re refueling tanker, which indicates that they were keeping them up in the air for quite some time, either to find where these folks were or to carry out in fact commando operations on the ground to retrieve them. So one of the two crew members from the F-15E, it has a pilot, it has a weapons technician, one of the two crew members was rescued. And you have to assume that was through a ground operation, special forces operation, but the other one is still missing. So, you know, we're in this uncertain period in terms of of the fate of of that service member. Uh, and you're absolutely right or Shawn Bell's absolutely right that in terms of the the images of this that if the Iranians have captured this service member and then he winds up on screen, first of all, it tries to bolster their idea of that they are not only surviving against the United States, that in a way that they're winning, that they're able to punch back. In terms of the American dimension, it immediately punches a hole in the narrative of Donald Trump and Pete Hexith, which is that American forces were unchallenged in the air, that there was no risk whatsoever. But then you're absolutely right to bring up that cultural memory that it invokes which goes back to 1979 you know and I grew up with this as a teenager that you know those 52 Americans that were taken in the US embassy held for 444 days you know and night after night you know we were told day 1, day 2, day three, day four. So it became like this thing imprinted on folks of a certain generation in the United States and indeed when you talk about Iraq as well, the fact that you had service personnel that at times were were captured by the Iraqis. So I I don't think it's like the downfall of the US war in Iran. The Trump folks are not going to stop because of this, but it it it sort of turns the narrative a bit. And from the military side, uh, it it raises the real questions about where they go next. Because let me add the second incident because I'm really interested about the second incident which hasn't gotten as much attention in the straight of Hormuz that vital waterway that the Iranians control. videos.title: Haverá uma invasão terrestre do Irã? (com o Coronel Larry Wilkerson) | The Chris Hedges Report
videos.description:
Enquanto os EUA se preparam para intensificar a guerra contra o Irã com uma possível invasão terrestre, o Coronel Wilkerson revela a longa relutância dos planejadores militares americanos em confrontar o Irã e a insensatez de tal empreendimento.

Apoie meu jornalismo independente no Substack: https://chrishedges.substack.com/

Siga o The Chris Hedges Report nas redes sociais: https://linktr.ee/chrishedges
videos.transcript: Pesquisar transcrição The contradictory statements emanating from the Trump White House about the war on Iran are evidence of panic. This panic caused by the inability to halt Iranian attacks, open the straight of Hormuz, or cope with a mounting global financial crisis means the Trump administration is now considering conducting ground operations, perhaps attempting to seize the Persian Gulf Islands of Abu Musa and the greater and lesser tombs, Lark and Car Islands. The United States has deployed the Tripoli Amphibious Ready Group with the 31st Marine Expeditionary Unit composed of about 3,500 sailors and marines in addition to transport and strike fighter aircraft as well as amphibious assault and tactical assets. It has deployed the boxer amphibious ready group with about 2500 Marines equipped with F35B lightning 2 stealth fighters, MV22B Osprey tilt rotors and attack helicopters. The US has also deployed 2,000 paratroopers of the first brigade combat team of the 82nd Airborne and is reportedly considering augmenting these forces with another 10,000 troops. What will a ground attack look like? How risky will be air or amphibious assaults? Will these assaults result in large casualty rates? Can they force Iran to capitulate? Will they reopen the straight of Hormuz to international shipping? What will such an assault, especially on Carg Island, which lies 16 miles off Iran's coast and processes 90% of the country's oil exports mean for the global economy? Or will a ground war rather than end the conflict widen and prolong it? Will we find ourselves sucked into a quagmire as we did in Iraq and Afghanistan? One that perhaps will end in humiliating defeat. If US troops land on one or more of these Iranian islands, how effective will Iran's anti-ship cruise missiles, ballistic missiles, underwater drones, and mines be in thwarting an assault or an occupation? The Abraham Lincoln carrier strike group has so far stayed out of range of Iran and operates in the Arabian Sea. Could it move closer to support an amphibious assault? Is an amphibious assault so risky that the only way to take these islands will be from the air? And if US forces did occupy one or more of these islands, what kind of damage could Iran's surfaceto-air missiles and air defense drones inflict? How would US forces on these islands obtain logistical support given the difficulty of maneuvering in the narrow straight? Joining me to discuss what may be the next stage of the war on Iran and its potential consequences is Lawrence Wilkerson, a retired Army Colonel and the former chief of staff to Secretary of State Colon Powell. He is a Vietnam War veteran who attended airborne school, Ranger School, and the Naval War College, and who is a helicopter pilot in Vietnam, logged over 1,000 hours on combat missions. He went on to serve as deputy director of the Marine Corps War College at Quantico, and was executive assistant to Admiral Steuart A. Ring, United States Navy Pacific Command, and Director of the United States Marine Corps War College. He has taught at the College of William and Mary and George Washington University. He is a senior fellow at the Eisenhower Media Network, a group of former military, intelligence, and civilian national security officials who describe themselves as offering alternative analyses untainted by Pentagon or defense industry ties and countering Washington's establishment narrative on most national security issues of the day. So before we begin with some of the specific questions that I raised in the introduction, just give us your view, Larry, of where we are with this war. That's difficult to do because the president and the secretary of war defense have not been very articulate in expressing what their mission is and what their purpose is. But let me back up for a moment and paint a little geostrategic landscape that I think is behind this war and is not talked about by many people primarily I think because they don't understand it historically or currently. We really are for some reason actually trying to stop China from executing its probably potentially at least most dangerous space road initiative to our interest. Now that assumes that it is enimical to our interest. I think we have made that assumption. That road would come up through Iran and trace Alexander the Great's route all the way up through the what would be the 1875 or so miles of shoreline on the Persian Gulf on the eastern side and all the way up to the Caucases. It would link China's Pacific ports just as the other three base road initiative railroads do through Russia and the Caucases with Europe in a way that would reduce its shipping time from something like a day and a half to two days depending on which way you go to about 16 hours. Trains have already pied these railroads including the one going through the southern part of Iran. If that happens, then all maritime nations, leading of which is the United States, of course, the protector of the seas, will no longer have seas really worth protecting in the sense of commerce because commerce will be basically landorn. At least 60% of it will come out of Asia and go to Europe and elsewhere on the land. Uh you'll have to get on some kind of boat to get to South America, I guess. But nonetheless, this is a monumental change and it's one we're fighting tooth and nail. Whether Donald Trump knows any of this or Pete Hathth knows any of this, I have no idea. But that is, I think, behind a lot of what we're doing. It's also behind Ukraine because Ukraine stopped the very central railroad which was already making 16-hour trips into the heart of Europe going all the way to Brimavan and La Hav and other places like that critical to European trade if it were not dumbbraided as it were by the war going on in Ukraine. So, and and think about that for a moment as to why Joe Biden and others Blinkin and Sullivan might have had some reason for doing Ukraine as it were rather than what they've been telling us or what they did tell us. This is all about China and it's all about stopping China. Which is to say, I'm not sure that any of this administration that is functioning in front of us every day knows anything about this other than that their masters have told them to do this. and we can talk about who their masters are back in the shadows, but I think that's ultimately what we're looking at. So, it's much more consequential in this struggle between the rising power and the declining power, and there's no question about that now, than people think or know. Uh and so it has these ramifications that make things that look tactically and even operationally stupid at the moment and yet are tied to this much bigger tapestry of geopolitical and geostrategic reality. Declining power, rising power. Where is Russia going to go in all this? Russia has a choice. She's both maritime power and land power principally the latter as Mckender made clear. Um, but she does have a formidable maritime capability now growing every moment by her Arctic coastline and the receding ice in the Arctic. So, she's got a choice to make and she hadn't made it yet. It looks like she's going with China, but there's this relationship between Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin, which is kind of strange really if you think about it. Unless you understand what Putin wants, which is a better relationship with Washington, and maybe even a helping hand. Not to Europe, not to NATO, not to the transatlantic link. he's destroyed those or is destroying them, but to Washington because he sees there's something left there worth being friendly with. So, we got to work all of that into what we're talking about right now. And what we're talking about right now is throwing a sledgehammer into that. That is awesome because what Iran is doing is upsetting everybody's plans to the tune of Muhammad bin Salman being so un incredibly insulted by Donald Trump in southern Florida recently that now he's thrown his lot in with Zalinski and has changed his routing of his pipelines and other things to avoid Israel and to go through Syria instead. I mean, these are monumental changes that this hugely, hugely wealthy sovereign wealth fund is making almost overnight because of the insults that Trump hurled at NBS. And then you've got Iran and what Iran can do to this whole business I've just talk I've been talking about in Southwest Asia, which is if it executes its second tier of targets. videos.title: O Irã é o principal patrocinador estatal do terrorismo? (com John Kiriakou) | The Chris Hedges Re...
videos.description:
Enquanto os EUA tentam rotular o Irã como um grande patrocinador do terrorismo de Estado, o denunciante da CIA, John Kiriakou, olha para dentro do próprio país, examinando as décadas de campanhas terroristas apoiadas pelos EUA e conduzidas com fins imperialistas.

Apoie meu jornalismo independente no Substack: https://chrishedges.substack.com/

Siga o The Chris Hedges Report nas redes sociais: https://linktr.ee/chrishedges

videos.title: Haverá uma invasão terrestre do Irã? (com o Coronel Larry Wilkerson) | The Chris Hedges Report
videos.description:
Enquanto os EUA se preparam para intensificar a guerra contra o Irã com uma possível invasão terrestre, o Coronel Wilkerson revela a longa relutância dos planejadores militares americanos em confrontar o Irã e a insensatez de tal empreendimento.

Apoie meu jornalismo independente no Substack: https://chrishedges.substack.com/

Siga o The Chris Hedges Report nas redes sociais: https://linktr.ee/chrishedges
videos.transcript: Pesquisar transcrição The contradictory statements emanating from the Trump White House about the war on Iran are evidence of panic. This panic caused by the inability to halt Iranian attacks, open the straight of Hormuz, or cope with a mounting global financial crisis means the Trump administration is now considering conducting ground operations, perhaps attempting to seize the Persian Gulf Islands of Abu Musa and the greater and lesser tombs, Lark and Car Islands. The United States has deployed the Tripoli Amphibious Ready Group with the 31st Marine Expeditionary Unit composed of about 3,500 sailors and marines in addition to transport and strike fighter aircraft as well as amphibious assault and tactical assets. It has deployed the boxer amphibious ready group with about 2500 Marines equipped with F35B lightning 2 stealth fighters, MV22B Osprey tilt rotors and attack helicopters. The US has also deployed 2,000 paratroopers of the first brigade combat team of the 82nd Airborne and is reportedly considering augmenting these forces with another 10,000 troops. What will a ground attack look like? How risky will be air or amphibious assaults? Will these assaults result in large casualty rates? Can they force Iran to capitulate? Will they reopen the straight of Hormuz to international shipping? What will such an assault, especially on Carg Island, which lies 16 miles off Iran's coast and processes 90% of the country's oil exports mean for the global economy? Or will a ground war rather than end the conflict widen and prolong it? Will we find ourselves sucked into a quagmire as we did in Iraq and Afghanistan? One that perhaps will end in humiliating defeat. If US troops land on one or more of these Iranian islands, how effective will Iran's anti-ship cruise missiles, ballistic missiles, underwater drones, and mines be in thwarting an assault or an occupation? The Abraham Lincoln carrier strike group has so far stayed out of range of Iran and operates in the Arabian Sea. Could it move closer to support an amphibious assault? Is an amphibious assault so risky that the only way to take these islands will be from the air? And if US forces did occupy one or more of these islands, what kind of damage could Iran's surfaceto-air missiles and air defense drones inflict? How would US forces on these islands obtain logistical support given the difficulty of maneuvering in the narrow straight? Joining me to discuss what may be the next stage of the war on Iran and its potential consequences is Lawrence Wilkerson, a retired Army Colonel and the former chief of staff to Secretary of State Colon Powell. He is a Vietnam War veteran who attended airborne school, Ranger School, and the Naval War College, and who is a helicopter pilot in Vietnam, logged over 1,000 hours on combat missions. He went on to serve as deputy director of the Marine Corps War College at Quantico, and was executive assistant to Admiral Steuart A. Ring, United States Navy Pacific Command, and Director of the United States Marine Corps War College. He has taught at the College of William and Mary and George Washington University. He is a senior fellow at the Eisenhower Media Network, a group of former military, intelligence, and civilian national security officials who describe themselves as offering alternative analyses untainted by Pentagon or defense industry ties and countering Washington's establishment narrative on most national security issues of the day. So before we begin with some of the specific questions that I raised in the introduction, just give us your view, Larry, of where we are with this war. That's difficult to do because the president and the secretary of war defense have not been very articulate in expressing what their mission is and what their purpose is. But let me back up for a moment and paint a little geostrategic landscape that I think is behind this war and is not talked about by many people primarily I think because they don't understand it historically or currently. We really are for some reason actually trying to stop China from executing its probably potentially at least most dangerous space road initiative to our interest. Now that assumes that it is enimical to our interest. I think we have made that assumption. That road would come up through Iran and trace Alexander the Great's route all the way up through the what would be the 1875 or so miles of shoreline on the Persian Gulf on the eastern side and all the way up to the Caucases. It would link China's Pacific ports just as the other three base road initiative railroads do through Russia and the Caucases with Europe in a way that would reduce its shipping time from something like a day and a half to two days depending on which way you go to about 16 hours. Trains have already pied these railroads including the one going through the southern part of Iran. If that happens, then all maritime nations, leading of which is the United States, of course, the protector of the seas, will no longer have seas really worth protecting in the sense of commerce because commerce will be basically landorn. At least 60% of it will come out of Asia and go to Europe and elsewhere on the land. Uh you'll have to get on some kind of boat to get to South America, I guess. But nonetheless, this is a monumental change and it's one we're fighting tooth and nail. Whether Donald Trump knows any of this or Pete Hathth knows any of this, I have no idea. But that is, I think, behind a lot of what we're doing. It's also behind Ukraine because Ukraine stopped the very central railroad which was already making 16-hour trips into the heart of Europe going all the way to Brimavan and La Hav and other places like that critical to European trade if it were not dumbbraided as it were by the war going on in Ukraine. So, and and think about that for a moment as to why Joe Biden and others Blinkin and Sullivan might have had some reason for doing Ukraine as it were rather than what they've been telling us or what they did tell us. This is all about China and it's all about stopping China. Which is to say, I'm not sure that any of this administration that is functioning in front of us every day knows anything about this other than that their masters have told them to do this. and we can talk about who their masters are back in the shadows, but I think that's ultimately what we're looking at. So, it's much more consequential in this struggle between the rising power and the declining power, and there's no question about that now, than people think or know. Uh and so it has these ramifications that make things that look tactically and even operationally stupid at the moment and yet are tied to this much bigger tapestry of geopolitical and geostrategic reality. videos.title: O Irã é o principal patrocinador estatal do terrorismo? (com John Kiriakou) | The Chris Hedges Re...
videos.description:
Enquanto os EUA tentam rotular o Irã como um grande patrocinador do terrorismo de Estado, o denunciante da CIA, John Kiriakou, olha para dentro do próprio país, examinando as décadas de campanhas terroristas apoiadas pelos EUA e conduzidas com fins imperialistas.

Apoie meu jornalismo independente no Substack: https://chrishedges.substack.com/

Siga o The Chris Hedges Report nas redes sociais: https://linktr.ee/chrishedges

videos.transcript: Pesquisar transcrição Thank you for inviting me. I um have just finished uh with the cartoonist Joe Sako. I hope you know his work Palestine footnotes in Gaza, a book on the genocide. We interviewed 29 families in Egypt from Gaza uh and have spent the last year writing the story of the genocide through their experiences. We literally turned in the manuscript a week ago, which is why if I look burned out, I am burned out. It'll be published um in uh October. Um but I didn't want to speak about the book given what's happening as I speak in southern Lebanon, in Iran, and in Gaza. Um I wanted to speak a little more globally today. The genocide in Gaza is the beginning. Welcome to the new world order, the age of technologically advanced barbarism. There are no rules for the strong, only for the weak. Oppose the strong. Refuse to bow to its capricious demands. And you are showered with missiles and bombs. We watch this madness daily with the war on Iran, the saturation bombing of southern Lebanon, and the suffering in Gaza. International bodies such as the United Nations have been neutered, transformed into useless appendages of another age. The sanctity of individual rights, open borders, and international law have vanished. the most psychopathic rulers of human history. Those who reduced cities to ashes, herded captive populations to execution sites, and littered lands they occupied with mass graves and corpses, have returned with a vengeance, opening up a vast moral abyss. The law, despite a few valiant efforts by a handful of judges who will soon be purged domestically and an international bodies such as the International Court of Justice is contemptuously violated. Savagery abroad, savagery at home. The BBC's Lucy Williamson reports that Israel is destroying South Lebanon, and I quote, "Using Gaza as a model, a blueprint for destruction, used again as a path to peace." End quote. Over 1 million people have already been displaced in Lebanon. 1ifth of the entire population of a country that already hosts the world's highest number of refugees per capita in just a few weeks. Add to this 2 million displaced in Gaza and 3 million displaced in Iran. 6 million people rendered homeless for four decades. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has been lobbying for the US to go to war with Iran. Previous administrations, Republican and Democrat, have refused in no small part because of fierce opposition within the Pentagon, which did not view Iran as an existential threat and did not project a positive outcome for the United States or its allies. But Donald Trump, encouraged by his inept negotiating team of his son-in-law Jared Kushner and fellow real estate developer and golfing partner Steve Witkoff, each fervent Zionists, took the bait. Joseph Kent, who resigned his position as director of the National Counterterrorism Center to protest the war, wrote in his resignation letter that quote, "Iran posed no imminent threat to our nation, and it is clear that we started this war due to pressure from Israel and its powerful American lobby. The public rationale for the war on Iran since it began on February 28th has been protein. Is it to shut down Iran's nuclear program? Is it to thwart Iran's ballistic missile program? videos.title: Haverá uma invasão terrestre do Irã? (com o Coronel Larry Wilkerson) | The Chris Hedges Report
videos.description:
Enquanto os EUA se preparam para intensificar a guerra contra o Irã com uma possível invasão terrestre, o Coronel Wilkerson revela a longa relutância dos planejadores militares americanos em confrontar o Irã e a insensatez de tal empreendimento.

Apoie meu jornalismo independente no Substack: https://chrishedges.substack.com/

Siga o The Chris Hedges Report nas redes sociais: https://linktr.ee/chrishedges
videos.transcript: Pesquisar transcrição The contradictory statements emanating from the Trump White House about the war on Iran are evidence of panic. This panic caused by the inability to halt Iranian attacks, open the straight of Hormuz, or cope with a mounting global financial crisis means the Trump administration is now considering conducting ground operations, perhaps attempting to seize the Persian Gulf Islands of Abu Musa and the greater and lesser tombs, Lark and Car Islands. The United States has deployed the Tripoli Amphibious Ready Group with the 31st Marine Expeditionary Unit composed of about 3,500 sailors and marines in addition to transport and strike fighter aircraft as well as amphibious assault and tactical assets. It has deployed the boxer amphibious ready group with about 2500 Marines equipped with F35B lightning 2 stealth fighters, MV22B Osprey tilt rotors and attack helicopters. The US has also deployed 2,000 paratroopers of the first brigade combat team of the 82nd Airborne and is reportedly considering augmenting these forces with another 10,000 troops. What will a ground attack look like? How risky will be air or amphibious assaults? Will these assaults result in large casualty rates? Can they force Iran to capitulate? Will they reopen the straight of Hormuz to international shipping? What will such an assault, especially on Carg Island, which lies 16 miles off Iran's coast and processes 90% of the country's oil exports mean for the global economy? Or will a ground war rather than end the conflict widen and prolong it? Will we find ourselves sucked into a quagmire as we did in Iraq and Afghanistan? One that perhaps will end in humiliating defeat. If US troops land on one or more of these Iranian islands, how effective will Iran's anti-ship cruise missiles, ballistic missiles, underwater drones, and mines be in thwarting an assault or an occupation? The Abraham Lincoln carrier strike group has so far stayed out of range of Iran and operates in the Arabian Sea. Could it move closer to support an amphibious assault? Is an amphibious assault so risky that the only way to take these islands will be from the air? And if US forces did occupy one or more of these islands, what kind of damage could Iran's surfaceto-air missiles and air defense drones inflict? How would US forces on these islands obtain logistical support given the difficulty of maneuvering in the narrow straight? Joining me to discuss what may be the next stage of the war on Iran and its potential consequences is Lawrence Wilkerson, a retired Army Colonel and the former chief of staff to Secretary of State Colon Powell. He is a Vietnam War veteran who attended airborne school, Ranger School, and the Naval War College, and who is a helicopter pilot in Vietnam, logged over 1,000 hours on combat missions. He went on to serve as deputy director of the Marine Corps War College at Quantico, and was executive assistant to Admiral Steuart A. Ring, United States Navy Pacific Command, and Director of the United States Marine Corps War College. He has taught at the College of William and Mary and George Washington University. He is a senior fellow at the Eisenhower Media Network, a group of former military, intelligence, and civilian national security officials who describe themselves as offering alternative analyses untainted by Pentagon or defense industry ties and countering Washington's establishment narrative on most national security issues of the day. So before we begin with some of the specific questions that I raised in the introduction, just give us your view, Larry, of where we are with this war. That's difficult to do because the president and the secretary of war defense have not been very articulate in expressing what their mission is and what their purpose is. But let me back up for a moment and paint a little geostrategic landscape that I think is behind this war and is not talked about by many people primarily I think because they don't understand it historically or currently. We really are for some reason actually trying to stop China from executing its probably potentially at least most dangerous space road initiative to our interest. Now that assumes that it is enimical to our interest. I think we have made that assumption. That road would come up through Iran and trace Alexander the Great's route all the way up through the what would be the 1875 or so miles of shoreline on the Persian Gulf on the eastern side and all the way up to the Caucases. It would link China's Pacific ports just as the other three base road initiative railroads do through Russia and the Caucases with Europe in a way that would reduce its shipping time from something like a day and a half to two days depending on which way you go to about 16 hours. Trains have already pied these railroads including the one going through the southern part of Iran. If that happens, then all maritime nations, leading of which is the United States, of course, the protector of the seas, will no longer have seas really worth protecting in the sense of commerce because commerce will be basically landorn. At least 60% of it will come out of Asia and go to Europe and elsewhere on the land. Uh you'll have to get on some kind of boat to get to South America, I guess. But nonetheless, this is a monumental change and it's one we're fighting tooth and nail. Whether Donald Trump knows any of this or Pete Hathth knows any of this, I have no idea. But that is, I think, behind a lot of what we're doing. It's also behind Ukraine because Ukraine stopped the very central railroad which was already making 16-hour trips into the heart of Europe going all the way to Brimavan and La Hav and other places like that critical to European trade if it were not dumbbraided as it were by the war going on in Ukraine. So, and and think about that for a moment as to why Joe Biden and others Blinkin and Sullivan might have had some reason for doing Ukraine as it were rather than what they've been telling us or what they did tell us. This is all about China and it's all about stopping China. Which is to say, I'm not sure that any of this administration that is functioning in front of us every day knows anything about this other than that their masters have told them to do this. and we can talk about who their masters are back in the shadows, but I think that's ultimately what we're looking at. videos.title: O Irã é o principal patrocinador estatal do terrorismo? (com John Kiriakou) | The Chris Hedges Re...
videos.description:
Enquanto os EUA tentam rotular o Irã como um grande patrocinador do terrorismo de Estado, o denunciante da CIA, John Kiriakou, olha para dentro do próprio país, examinando as décadas de campanhas terroristas apoiadas pelos EUA e conduzidas com fins imperialistas.

Apoie meu jornalismo independente no Substack: https://chrishedges.substack.com/

Siga o The Chris Hedges Report nas redes sociais: https://linktr.ee/chrishedges

videos.transcript: Pesquisar transcrição Thank you for inviting me. I um have just finished uh with the cartoonist Joe Sako. I hope you know his work Palestine footnotes in Gaza, a book on the genocide. We interviewed 29 families in Egypt from Gaza uh and have spent the last year writing the story of the genocide through their experiences. We literally turned in the manuscript a week ago, which is why if I look burned out, I am burned out. It'll be published um in uh October. Um but I didn't want to speak about the book given what's happening as I speak in southern Lebanon, in Iran, and in Gaza. Um I wanted to speak a little more globally today. The genocide in Gaza is the beginning. Welcome to the new world order, the age of technologically advanced barbarism. There are no rules for the strong, only for the weak. Oppose the strong. Refuse to bow to its capricious demands. And you are showered with missiles and bombs. We watch this madness daily with the war on Iran, the saturation bombing of southern Lebanon, and the suffering in Gaza. International bodies such as the United Nations have been neutered, transformed into useless appendages of another age. The sanctity of individual rights, open borders, and international law have vanished. the most psychopathic rulers of human history. Those who reduced cities to ashes, herded captive populations to execution sites, and littered lands they occupied with mass graves and corpses, have returned with a vengeance, opening up a vast moral abyss. The law, despite a few valiant efforts by a handful of judges who will soon be purged domestically and an international bodies such as the International Court of Justice is contemptuously violated. Savagery abroad, savagery at home. The BBC's Lucy Williamson reports that Israel is destroying South Lebanon, and I quote, "Using Gaza as a model, a blueprint for destruction, used again as a path to peace." End quote. Over 1 million people have already been displaced in Lebanon. 1ifth of the entire population of a country that already hosts the world's highest number of refugees per capita in just a few weeks. Add to this 2 million displaced in Gaza and 3 million displaced in Iran. 6 million people rendered homeless for four decades. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has been lobbying for the US to go to war with Iran. Previous administrations, Republican and Democrat, have refused in no small part because of fierce opposition within the Pentagon, which did not view Iran as an existential threat and did not project a positive outcome for the United States or its allies. But Donald Trump, encouraged by his inept negotiating team of his son-in-law Jared Kushner and fellow real estate developer and golfing partner Steve Witkoff, each fervent Zionists, took the bait. Joseph Kent, who resigned his position as director of the National Counterterrorism Center to protest the war, wrote in his resignation letter that quote, "Iran posed no imminent threat to our nation, and it is clear that we started this war due to pressure from Israel and its powerful American lobby. The public rationale for the war on Iran since it began on February 28th has been protein. Is it to shut down Iran's nuclear program? Is it to thwart Iran's ballistic missile program? Is it because the US carried out preemptive attacks on Iran, as Marco Rubio said, to ensure the safety of US assets once Israel decided to strike? Is it because the Iranian government carried out lethal repression, killing hundreds of anti-government protesters during massive street protests? Is it regime change? Is it an attempt to shut down Iran's so-called state sponsored terrorism? Or are these subuses for something else? Certainly, Israel and the US seek regime change, but here it appears the US and Israel diverge. Israel also apparently seeks, as in Iraq, Syria, Libya, and Lebanon, the physical disintegration of Iran, the breaking apart of the country into waring ethnic and religious enclaves, the transformation of Iran into a failed state. Persians in Iran, where I worked frequently, constitute roughly 61% of the population with various minority groups who often suffer state repression making up the remaining 39%. These ethnic groups include Azerbaijanis, Kurds, Lures, Balaks, Arabs, and Turkmans along with religious minorities such as Sunnis, Christians, Bahigh, Zoroastrians, and Jews. The shattering of Iran into antagonistic ethnic and religious enclaves would leave Israel as the dominant power in the region, given it the ability to, if not occupy its neighbors uh then directly control and subjugate them through proxies, part of a longheld desire for greater Israel. It would also make it possible for foreign states to control Iranian gas reserves. the second largest in the world and its oil reserves 12% of the global total. Israel's crusade against the Palestinians, the Lebanese, and now the Iranians is justified by the extermination of 6 million Jews during the Holocaust. But it is not lost on the global south, especially Palestinians, that nearly all Holocaust scholars have refused to condemn the genocide in Gaza. Not one of the institutions dedicated to researching and commemorating the Holocaust have drawn the obvious historical parallels or decrieded the mass slaughter. Holocaust scholars, with a handful of exceptions, have exposed their true purpose, which is not to examine the dark side of human nature and the frightening propensity we all have to commit evil, but to sanctify Jews as eternal victims and absolve the ethnationalist state of Israel of its crimes of settler colonialism, apartheid, and genocide. The hijacking of the Holocaust, the failure to defend Palestinian victims because they are Palestinian has imploded the moral authority of Holocaust scholars and Holocaust memorials. They have been exposed at ve as vehicles not to prevent genocide but to perpetuate it, not to explore the past but manipulate the present. any tepeeid recognition that the Holocaust may not be the exclusive property of Israel and its Zionist supporters is swiftly shut down. videos.title: Haverá uma invasão terrestre do Irã? (com o Coronel Larry Wilkerson) | The Chris Hedges Report
videos.description:
Enquanto os EUA se preparam para intensificar a guerra contra o Irã com uma possível invasão terrestre, o Coronel Wilkerson revela a longa relutância dos planejadores militares americanos em confrontar o Irã e a insensatez de tal empreendimento.

Apoie meu jornalismo independente no Substack: https://chrishedges.substack.com/

Siga o The Chris Hedges Report nas redes sociais: https://linktr.ee/chrishedges
videos.transcript: Pesquisar transcrição The contradictory statements emanating from the Trump White House about the war on Iran are evidence of panic. This panic caused by the inability to halt Iranian attacks, open the straight of Hormuz, or cope with a mounting global financial crisis means the Trump administration is now considering conducting ground operations, perhaps attempting to seize the Persian Gulf Islands of Abu Musa and the greater and lesser tombs, Lark and Car Islands. The United States has deployed the Tripoli Amphibious Ready Group with the 31st Marine Expeditionary Unit composed of about 3,500 sailors and marines in addition to transport and strike fighter aircraft as well as amphibious assault and tactical assets. It has deployed the boxer amphibious ready group with about 2500 Marines equipped with F35B lightning 2 stealth fighters, MV22B Osprey tilt rotors and attack helicopters. The US has also deployed 2,000 paratroopers of the first brigade combat team of the 82nd Airborne and is reportedly considering augmenting these forces with another 10,000 troops. What will a ground attack look like? How risky will be air or amphibious assaults? Will these assaults result in large casualty rates? Can they force Iran to capitulate? Will they reopen the straight of Hormuz to international shipping? What will such an assault, especially on Carg Island, which lies 16 miles off Iran's coast and processes 90% of the country's oil exports mean for the global economy? Or will a ground war rather than end the conflict widen and prolong it? Will we find ourselves sucked into a quagmire as we did in Iraq and Afghanistan? One that perhaps will end in humiliating defeat. If US troops land on one or more of these Iranian islands, how effective will Iran's anti-ship cruise missiles, ballistic missiles, underwater drones, and mines be in thwarting an assault or an occupation? The Abraham Lincoln carrier strike group has so far stayed out of range of Iran and operates in the Arabian Sea. Could it move closer to support an amphibious assault? Is an amphibious assault so risky that the only way to take these islands will be from the air? And if US forces did occupy one or more of these islands, what kind of damage could Iran's surfaceto-air missiles and air defense drones inflict? How would US forces on these islands obtain logistical support given the difficulty of maneuvering in the narrow straight? Joining me to discuss what may be the next stage of the war on Iran and its potential consequences is Lawrence Wilkerson, a retired Army Colonel and the former chief of staff to Secretary of State Colon Powell. He is a Vietnam War veteran who attended airborne school, Ranger School, and the Naval War College, and who is a helicopter pilot in Vietnam, logged over 1,000 hours on combat missions. He went on to serve as deputy director of the Marine Corps War College at Quantico, and was executive assistant to Admiral Steuart A. Ring, United States Navy Pacific Command, and Director of the United States Marine Corps War College. He has taught at the College of William and Mary and George Washington University. He is a senior fellow at the Eisenhower Media Network, a group of former military, intelligence, and civilian national security officials who describe themselves as offering alternative analyses untainted by Pentagon or defense industry ties and countering Washington's establishment narrative on most national security issues of the day. So before we begin with some of the specific questions that I raised in the introduction, just give us your view, Larry, of where we are with this war. That's difficult to do because the president and the secretary of war defense have not been very articulate in expressing what their mission is and what their purpose is. But let me back up for a moment and paint a little geostrategic landscape that I think is behind this war and is not talked about by many people primarily I think because they don't understand it historically or currently. We really are for some reason actually trying to stop China from executing its probably potentially at least most dangerous space road initiative to our interest. Now that assumes that it is enimical to our interest. I think we have made that assumption. That road would come up through Iran and trace Alexander the Great's route all the way up through the what would be the 1875 or so miles of shoreline on the Persian Gulf on the eastern side and all the way up to the Caucases. It would link China's Pacific ports just as the other three base road initiative railroads do through Russia and the Caucases with Europe in a way that would reduce its shipping time from something like a day and a half to two days depending on which way you go to about 16 hours. Trains have already pied these railroads including the one going through the southern part of Iran. If that happens, then all maritime nations, leading of which is the United States, of course, the protector of the seas, will no longer have seas really worth protecting in the sense of commerce because commerce will be basically landorn. At least 60% of it will come out of Asia and go to Europe and elsewhere on the land. Uh you'll have to get on some kind of boat to get to South America, I guess. But nonetheless, this is a monumental change and it's one we're fighting tooth and nail. Whether Donald Trump knows any of this or Pete Hathth knows any of this, I have no idea. So, and and think about that for a moment as to why Joe Biden and others Blinkin and Sullivan might have had some reason for doing Ukraine as it were rather than what they've been telling us or what they did tell us. This is all about China and it's all about stopping China. videos.title: O Irã é o principal patrocinador estatal do terrorismo? (com John Kiriakou) | The Chris Hedges Re...
videos.description:
Enquanto os EUA tentam rotular o Irã como um grande patrocinador do terrorismo de Estado, o denunciante da CIA, John Kiriakou, olha para dentro do próprio país, examinando as décadas de campanhas terroristas apoiadas pelos EUA e conduzidas com fins imperialistas.

Apoie meu jornalismo independente no Substack: https://chrishedges.substack.com/

Siga o The Chris Hedges Report nas redes sociais: https://linktr.ee/chrishedges

The hijacking of the Holocaust, the failure to defend Palestinian victims because they are Palestinian has imploded the moral authority of Holocaust scholars and Holocaust memorials. Levy was a fierce critic of the apartheid state of Israel and its treatment of Palestinians.
